one_line_takeaway: PFCR shows you can do cross-domain sequential recommendation across NON-overlapping, privacy-siloed domains by federating only text-derived semantic item-code gradients (never user data), encrypting them with quantization + randomized response, and adapting per domain with soft prompts -- beating both single-domain and prior text-only recommenders.
key_finding: On both dataset pairs PFCR beats all four baseline families and, notably, its own strongest non-federated competitor VQRec, while never sharing user data across domains. Gains are largest on the sparser cross-platform OnlineRetail-Pantry pair (e.g. Pantry Recall@10 0.0620 for PFCR(l) vs 0.0561 for VQRec, ~10.5% relative). The federated aggregation choice matters: plain FedAvg outperforms FedProx, Scaffold, and a 'PopCode' variant that only aggregates high-frequency codes, and does so with lower communication cost. Ablations confirm each component (VQ item representation, federated content representation, domain-adaptive prompting) contributes, and hyperparameter analysis shows more federated rounds t are NOT monotonically better (excess updates hurt), while performance is relatively insensitive to epsilon because randomized response perturbs only part of the gradient rather than all of it like Gaussian/Laplace noise.
core_contribution: The paper proposes PFCR (Prompt-enhanced Federated Content Representation), a two-stage framework for Domain-level Privacy-preserving Cross-domain Sequential Recommendation (DPCSR) in the realistic setting where users and items are entirely NON-overlapping across domains. Its own novel pieces are: (1) representing items not by atomic IDs but via a 'text -> code -> representation' pipeline (BERT-encoded description text mapped to discrete codes via Product Quantization, then looked up in a shared global code embedding table), so items from different domains live in one semantic space; (2) a federated content-representation pre-training schema that treats each DOMAIN as a client and, crucially, uploads ONLY the code-embedding (item) gradients to the server while strictly prohibiting any user-related gradients, aggregated with FedAvg; (3) a composite gradient-encryption method combining quantization and randomized response (a local-DP style perturbation with privacy parameter epsilon) to defend the uploaded gradients; and (4) two domain-adaptive prompt fine-tuning strategies -- 'full prompting' (domain prompt + user prompt) and 'light prompting' (domain prompt only) -- to adapt the pre-trained model to a target domain. Note: the 'text->code->representation' VQ item idea and the base semantic-ID approach are explicitly credited to prior work (Hou et al. VQRec [12] and UniSRec [13]); PFCR's contribution is porting this into a federated, non-overlapping, prompt-tuned setting, not inventing the VQ item representation itself.
threat_model: Adversaries considered: (a) the central aggregation server and 'untrusted partners' who could inspect uploaded gradients to infer private information; (b) external attackers intercepting gradients in transit and reconstructing item/user information via known gradient-inversion/membership attacks; (c) organizations attempting de-anonymization by identifying overlapping users across domains to infer their preferences elsewhere. Protected asset = users' interaction sequences (treated as private); item description texts are treated as PUBLIC. Defenses: user embeddings and user-related parameters/gradients are never uploaded (only item code-embedding gradients are); those gradients are clipped, quantized into b=2^k buckets, and perturbed by randomized response with Bernoulli flipping governed by privacy budget epsilon; and the non-overlapping design removes any need to share or align user identities across domains. The framework offers empirical/DP-style protection, not formal cryptographic guarantees, and the paper does not run explicit attack simulations to quantify residual leakage.
limitations: Only two domain pairs and exactly two clients/domains are tested, so scalability to many clients is unshown. The privacy protection is argued qualitatively (LDP-style randomized response + withholding user gradients) but there is NO empirical attack (e.g. gradient-inversion or membership-inference) run against PFCR to measure actual leakage, and no formal privacy-utility bound is proven. Absolute accuracy remains low (Recall@10 ~0.06 on Pantry), reflecting task difficulty. The method assumes item description texts are safe to treat as fully public. There is a notational ambiguity in the paper between the code-table symbols M/D and M_c/D (Section 3.3 vs implementation details). Improvements over VQRec, though marked statistically significant (t-test p<.05), are numerically small on the Amazon pairs (e.g. Office Recall@10 0.1215 vs 0.1207).

datasets_used: Office-Arts (Amazon 'Office' and 'Arts' domains, non-overlapping cross-domain pair)
OnlineRetail-Pantry (cross-platform pair: OnlineRetail e-commerce data + Amazon 'Pantry' domain)
quantitative_results: Office Recall@10: PFCR(l) 0.1215 (best), PFCR(f) 0.1206, vs best baseline VQRec 0.1207
Arts Recall@50: PFCR(l) 0.2208 (best), PFCR(f) 0.2199, vs VQRec 0.2160
Arts NDCG@10: PFCR(f) 0.0677 (best) vs FDSA 0.0671, VQRec 0.0624
Pantry Recall@10: PFCR(l) 0.0620 / PFCR(f) 0.0616 vs VQRec 0.0561 (~10% relative gain)
FL-strategy comparison (Office): FedAvg Recall@10 0.1213 > PopCode 0.1209 > FedProx 0.1197 > Scaffold 0.1146
Ablation (Office Recall@10): full PFCR 0.1206 vs PFCR-VFD 0.1157 (no VQIR/FCR/DP), PFCR-FD 0.1207, PFCR-D 0.1213, PFCR-F 0.1169

Hyperparameters: code table M=48,D=256 (Office-Arts); M=32,D=256 (OnlineRetail-Pantry); epsilon in [0.1,0.9]; buckets b in [2^6,2^10]; 10 federated rounds; hidden dim 300; 4 heads; batch 1024; lr 0.001
baselines_compared: FL-strategy baselines: FedProx, Scaffold, PopCode (vs the adopted FedAvg)
